name: Avocado
description: Avocado is toxic to cats primarily because of persin, a fungicidal compound the avocado tree produces as a natural defense. While humans metabolise persin safely, cats lack this ability. Persin triggers dangerous fluid accumulation (edema) in the lungs, chest cavity, abdomen, and around the heart, causing respiratory distress and, at high doses, myocardial necrosis (death of heart muscle tissue). The plant's extremely high fat content creates an additional hazard by triggering acute pancreatitis even when persin levels are low.
safetyNotes: Symptoms: Vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain; difficulty breathing and respiratory distress from lung fluid; lethargy, weakness, and abdominal swelling.
Physical hazard: The pit is a severe choking and obstruction risk if swallowed.

All parts are dangerous: Leaves carry the highest persin concentration and are considered most toxic; skin, bark, and pit also contain high levels. The fruit flesh contains only trace amounts of persin but is still risky due to its high fat content.

symptoms.name: Vomiting, Diarrhea, and Abdominal Discomfort
symptoms.onset: Clinical signs may be evident within 24 hours (usually after 12 hours).
symptoms.notes: Persin acts as a gastrointestinal irritant. Even small amounts of the fruit flesh can cause stomach upset, but the leaves, bark, skin, and pit contain higher concentrations of the toxin.Pancreatitis

symptoms.name: Pancreatitis (Inflammation of the Pancreas)
symptoms.onset: Variable; symptoms like abdominal pain and vomiting often appear within 24–72 hours after ingesting
symptoms.notes: This is triggered by the high dietary fat content in the avocado flesh and oil rather than the persin toxin itself. It can lead to dehydration, abdominal swelling, and organ damage.Respiratory and Cardiac Distress

symptoms.name: Labored Breathing (Dyspnea), Coughing, and Fluid Accumulation (Edema/Congestion)
symptoms.notes: In severe cases, persin toxicity can cause damage to heart muscle fibers, leading to fluid accumulation around the heart (hydropericardium) and in the lungs (pulmonary congestion). While cats are less sensitive to this than birds, it is a documented risk for dogs and cats in high doses.Foreign Body

symptoms.name: Choking or Intestinal Blockage
symptoms.onset: Immediate (if choking) to days (if intestinal blockage occurs).
symptoms.notes: The large pit (seed) and thick skin pose a physical danger. If swallowed, the pit can block the esophagus, stomach, or intestines, requiring surgical removal.Lethargy and Weakness

symptoms.name: Lethargy and Depression
symptoms.notes: The cat may appear tired, inactive, or "dull" as a general systemic reaction to the toxin or dehydration from vomiting.

symptoms.name: Fluid accumulation around the heart and myocardial damage
